name: Gold Standard Removal
description: The removal of the gold standard in the United States, particularly the final severing in 1971 under President Nixon, is a complex economic and political event that has spawned numerous theories and interpretations, some bordering on conspiracy. Proponents of these theories often view the abandonment of the gold standard as a deliberate act by powerful elites (e.g., international bankers, the Federal Reserve) to manipulate the economy, increase inflation, and consolidate their wealth and power at the expense of ordinary citizens. They argue that the gold standard provided a natural check on government spending and monetary policy, preventing excessive debt and inflation. They see the post-gold standard era as one of unchecked government power and economic instability. The origins of these theories can be traced back to populist movements of the late 19th and early 20th centuries that were critical of centralized banking and finance. Key pieces of 'evidence' often cited include historical inflation rates, the increasing national debt, and the perceived erosion of the dollar's purchasing power since 1971. Some proponents link the removal to secret societies or international organizations allegedly seeking to establish a New World Order. Mainstream economic perspective views the removal of the gold standard as a necessary step to provide the government with the flexibility to manage the economy during times of crisis. Economists argue that the gold standard limited the government's ability to respond to recessions and that it was unsustainable in a globalized economy. They point to the limitations and problems associated with the gold standard in the pre-1971 era, such as its inflexibility and its inability to effectively address economic shocks. Critics of the conspiracy theories also highlight the complex factors contributing to inflation and economic instability, arguing that these cannot be solely attributed to the absence of the gold standard and that attributing them to a single cause oversimplifies complex economic realities.
subtitle: End of Gold-Backed Dollar System

claims.claim: The gold standard removal was a deliberate plot by elites to control the economy.
claims.evidence: Increased national debt, inflation rates since 1971, perceived erosion of purchasing power.
claims.criticism: Economists argue complex factors contribute to economic instability and government flexibility is needed.

claims.claim: The Federal Reserve benefits from the absence of the gold standard.
claims.evidence: The Fed's ability to print money and influence interest rates without gold backing.
claims.criticism: The Fed's actions are subject to oversight and are intended to stabilize the economy, not enrich itself.

claims.claim: The gold standard provided economic stability and prevented excessive government spending.
claims.evidence: Historical examples of periods under the gold standard with lower inflation.
claims.criticism: The gold standard also caused economic inflexibility and exacerbated recessions.

keyFigures.name: Ron Paul
keyFigures.role: Advocate for returning to the gold standard.

keyFigures.name: Milton Friedman
keyFigures.role: Advocate for floating exchange rates and critic of the gold standard.

timeline.event: President Roosevelt restricts private gold ownership.
Bretton Woods Agreement establishes the dollar as the world's reserve currency, pegged to gold.
President Nixon ends the convertibility of the dollar to gold, effectively ending the gold standard.

sources.author: Eichengreen, Barry
sources.work: Golden Fetters: The Gold Standard and the Great Depression, 1919-1939
sources.title: Eichengreen, Barry
